title: Gift of Discernment (Part 1)
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: Gift of Discernment (Part 1) - 1 Corinthians 12_1-7 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Christ Gifts His Church Through the Spirit
subpoints: Every way Christ provides for his church, he does so through gifting his people (1 Corinthians 12:1-7)
Ephesians 4:7-8: grace given to each according to the measure of Christ’s gift
Spiritual gifts build a web of love and service that cannot be undone
Three principles: variety of gifts, all empowered by the Spirit, given in all believers

outline.title: What Is the Gift of Discernment?
subpoints: All Christians are called to practice discernment, but some are specially gifted by God
The gift is the ability to look at a teaching, proposition, or statement and determine if it is from God
It is always Bible-driven: comparing what is taught to the revealed Word of God
Satan can appear as an angel of light; gifted discerners identify counterfeit teachings and leaders

outline.title: Discernment and Church Leadership
subpoints: Elders must have the gift of discernment (Titus 1: able to refute heresy; 1 Timothy 3: able to teach doctrine)
The Spirit equips men with this gift when they are called to eldership
The gift is implied in the qualifications, not a separate credential

outline.title: How to Know If You Have the Gift
subpoints: Begin with prayer: ask God to make it plain (James 1:5)
Look for passion: what problems or needs do you keep noticing?
Ask others: the community often recognizes gifts before the individual does
Try different opportunities to serve and keep practicing

outline.title: Seven Practical Uses of the Gift of Discernment
subpoints: Separating truth from error in teaching and doctrine
Helping the body of Christ apply the Bible to life and understand the will of God
Finding the presence of the Holy Spirit’s work and warning against counterfeit teaching
Identifying worldliness (including legalistic extra-biblical standards)
Overseeing the exercise of spiritual gifts in the church
Settling disputes within the church (1 Corinthians 6)
Protecting new and immature Christians as they grow in faith

outline.title: Conclusion: What If I Don’t Have This Gift?
subpoints: All Christians are still called to grow in discernment, even without the specific gift
The absence of the gift does not excuse a believer from being discerning
Those with the gift should exercise it in service; those without should support and learn from them
The gift is especially urgent in an age when Christianity is culturally acceptable and persecution is low

summary:
In this first of a two-part Sunday school lesson on the gift of discernment, Pastor Tito Lyro opens in 1 Corinthians 12:1-7 and establishes that Christ provides for his church in every way through the spiritual gifts he bestows on his people by the Holy Spirit. Three foundational principles emerge from the text: there is a variety of gifts (and the church needs them all), all gifts are empowered by the Spirit rather than being mere personality traits, and every believer has been given at least one spiritual gift. The gifts exist for the common good of the body, so that the whole church might grow into the fullness of Christ its head.

Pastor Lyro then defines the gift of discernment specifically: it is the Spirit-given ability to examine a teaching, a claim, or an appearance and determine whether it is from God or not. Crucially, this gift is always anchored in the Word of God—a person cannot claim to have the gift of discernment while remaining indifferent to Scripture. Because elders are required to refute heresy and teach sound doctrine (Titus 1; 1 Timothy 3), the gift of discernment is implicitly required for church leadership. The lesson notes that in a comfortable, non-persecuted age like contemporary America, the gift is of urgent importance, because false teaching thrives when it costs nothing to be a Christian teacher.

The lesson offers practical guidance for identifying whether one has this gift: pray for wisdom (James 1:5), look for passion toward a particular need, seek feedback from others in the community, and simply try serving in ways that call for discernment. The class discussion reveals lively engagement on how the community recognizes gifts—including a humorous interjection about a “gift of sarcasm.”

Seven concrete applications of the gift close the lesson: separating truth from error, helping the church understand the will of God, discerning where the Spirit is genuinely at work, identifying worldliness (including legalism as a form of worldliness), overseeing the use of other spiritual gifts, settling internal church disputes rather than going to civil courts, and protecting newer Christians. The session ends with a Q&A and a reminder that all Christians should pursue discernment—even those who do not possess it as a primary gift.
transcript:
## Opening

We love to get to grab a Bible and turn to First Corinthians chapter 12 as we talk about the gift of the sermon. Thank you for Nick teaching last week. He's got really good things about his lesson. I heard that there was like 20 salvations and you know, a revival broke and people are speaking tons and so on. So, I'm glad for his teaching here last week. Last week we saw the place of discernment and the discerning of the will of God in your life. Today we're going to talk about the gift of discernment as a gift of the Spirit. Well, today and next week. We cleaned this last year too because it got a little too big. So today's part one, Lord willing next week, part two.

## Scripture Reading: 1 Corinthians 12:1-7

So, First Corinthians 12, we'll start in verse 1.

*Now, concerning spiritual gifts, brethren, I do not want you to be ignorant. You know that you were Gentiles, carried away to these dumb idols, however you were led. Therefore I make known to you that no one speaking by the Spirit of God calls Jesus accursed. And no one can say that Jesus is Lord except by the Holy Spirit. There are diversities of gifts but the same Spirit. There are differences of ministries but the same Lord and there are diversities of activities but it is the same God who works all in all. But the manifestation of the Spirit is given to each one for the profit of all.*

## Christ Gifts His Church

The Lord Jesus Christ provides for His church in every way. In every single way the Lord Jesus Christ provides for us or His church, He gifts His people. He gives spiritual gifts to His people so that everyone can grow in Him. Ephesians 4:7-8 says that to each one of us grace was given according to the measure of Christ's gift. Therefore He says when he ascended on high, he led captivity captive and gave gifts to men. So Christ gives gifts through His Spirit and the gifts of the Holy Spirit are meant to build within the local church a web of love and service that cannot be undone.

In the passage we read, we find a basic framework for thinking about spiritual gifts, especially verses 4 through 7. The first thing we see is that there is a variety of gifts. There's all kinds of gifts given to the church, not just one. And the whole point of the chapter is that we need them all. There isn't one that we don't need—because Paul is making the very point that some people are saying, "My gift is more important. We don't need that gift." He talks about the toes and the nose and the less noble parts of the body and the more noble parts of the body as people perceive them, all being needed.

So there's a variety of gifts because we need them all. We need the gifts that God's given us in order to build the church, to build each other up in the fullness of Christ. The second thing we see is that all these gifts are empowered by the Holy Spirit. So they're not just our own aptitudes. They're not just the things that we like doing. They're empowered by the Spirit. So it's not just a personality-driven thing. We also see here in this passage that they are in all believers. Every believer has been gifted by the Spirit.

One of the things I'm going to say today is that the Spirit might change your gift as you grow in the Lord. Because the gifts are given as the church needs them. So for a season the Lord might have you do one thing, and as the church changes or grows or shrinks or ages or gets younger, you might change your gift and you might have to serve him in a different way.

As an empowerment by the Spirit, spiritual gifts manifest the Spirit. If you're truly exercising a spiritual gift, it will cause people to exalt Christ—not to look at the person exercising the gift. The gifts are a manifestation of the Spirit, and the primary ministry of the Spirit is to exalt Christ. So in a church in which the Spirit is very active, Christ will be made much of.

## What Is the Gift of Discernment?

Now, what is the gift of spiritual discernment? Discernment is something that every Christian is called to practice—that is true. But we're going to see that some Christians have an easier and more accurate time of discerning things than others.

We read the Bible and read the New Testament and it's clear to us that there are many false prophets and false teachers who claim to speak God's words with God's authority. Throughout the New Testament, we have all these exhortations to test all teaching. See that in First John 4:1. We see that in Jesus himself saying the Spirit will lead you in what is true. Paul says to test all things, to verify all things.

The word for "tests" is about dividing and judging through what is right and what is wrong—which is very much the essence of our definition of discernment.

The gift of discernment is the ability to look at a teaching, look at a proposition, look at a statement, look at what's being taught, and figure out if this is true or not, if it is from God or not. That's really the ultimate definition of the gift of discernment.

All Christians are responsible for what they believe. Nobody can say, "He made me believe it."—that argument doesn't work. But it seems clear that some people are especially gifted by God for this task. Men and women with the gift of discernment are especially gifted in distinguishing between those words, deeds, and appearances that are true and those that are false.

I put the word "appearance" here because, remember what Paul says in Second Corinthians, that Satan sometimes appears as an angel of light. He's not necessarily talking about a physical sighting of Satan, but that his ideas can be packaged as something that appears to come from light. We have to discern those things so that we don't follow them.

Those Christians who are gifted with discernment will be able to compare what is presented against God's Word and expose the fraudulent and keep what is good. It's always Bible-driven. The Spirit's presence is where the Bible is being faithfully taught.

## Discernment and Church Eldership

This means that to be an elder in the Church of Jesus Christ, you must have the gift of discernment. It's implied in the qualifications found in Titus 1 and 1 Timothy 3. Both say that the elder must be able to refute heresy (Titus) and to teach the doctrine the church believes (Timothy). All those things have to do with discernment. The Spirit will equip men with that gift as they are called to be elders in the church.

## How to Know If You Have the Gift

How can I know that the Holy Spirit has gifted me with the gift of discernment?

The Bible seems to indicate that Christians would typically know how they have been gifted—God will help them know. And yet most Christians struggle to identify the specific ways God has gifted them.

The first thing you should do is begin with prayer. God promises to give wisdom to anyone who asks for it—James 1:5. So we should begin our search for gifting by asking God to make it plain to us how He has gifted us and how He desires that we serve Him by serving others.

Second, look for passion. What are you passionate about? If you're passionate about the truth of God, you may have the gift of discernment. A person passionate about having people into their home may have the gift of hospitality.

Ask others. That's actually a better indication than self-knowledge. Ask others what they think. Try it out—if I think I have that gift, let me try it out. See if I do. Try different opportunities to serve. Then keep on trying it. Don't give up.

The Bible doesn't tell us that all spiritual gifts are given at the time of conversion. Keep on growing and keep on trying and keep on growing in the Lord.

## Seven Ways the Gift of Discernment Is Exercised in the Church Today

What do I do with the gift of discernment if I have it? I serve others. Here are seven specific and practical ways this gift can be exercised in the church today.

**First**: Separating the truth from error. If you have the gift of discernment, you're able to separate the truth from error. A person with this gift will help others discern the will of God. The will of God is not a bull's-eye—it's more like a park, with the Word of God as the boundaries. Anywhere in that park, you're in the will of God.

**Second**: Helping the body of Christ find the presence and work of the Holy Spirit. People with the gift of discernment are finely tuned to the presence and work of the Holy Spirit, able on the basis of their knowledge of God to understand how He works and where He's working. They're also gifted in being able to tell where the Spirit is not present and warn others of counterfeit teaching or counterfeit Christians.

**Third**: A person with a gift of discernment is able to identify worldliness. Worldliness usually means conforming to the world in obvious sin. But another side of worldliness is having lists not biblical in origin of how a person should behave. That is also worldliness, because it's extra-biblical. The legalistic position is by definition a worldly position. A person with the gift of discernment is able to see that.

**Fourth**: A person with a gift of discernment should oversee the exercise of spiritual gifts in general. Help other people exercise their gifts. This is why it's important that elders truly have the gift of discernment—they are the ones overseeing all these things.

**Fifth**: The person with a gift of discernment should help the church decide its disputes. Remember 1 Corinthians 6? The big problem in Corinthians was that they were suing each other in civil court. Paul says, "You who are going to judge all the nations in the age to come, you can't take care of your own disputes in the church?" The church has been given everything it needs to settle its own disputes.

**Sixth**: They should use this gift to protect new Christians. The gift of discernment depends so much on knowledge of God's truth that the Christian with that gift should be careful to protect those who are new or immature in the faith.

**Seventh** (implied): This gift is of ultimate importance in our age. The most dangerous time for the church is a time in which she is not being persecuted, when it costs nothing to be a Christian teacher. Things rise up when there is no cost.

## What If I Don't Have This Gift?

All Christians are called to be discerning. Correct. But not everyone has the gift of discernment. Those with the gift should use it to serve the church in these seven ways. Those without should still pursue discernment, keep reading the Bible, keep growing, and seek help from those who are more discerning.

Don't leave here saying Pastor said I don't have to be discerning because I don't have the gift. What I'm saying is: if you have the gift, exercise it in these ways. If you don't, you still seek to be discerning and you still look at the scriptures and ask for help from people who may be more discerning than you.

## Closing Prayer

*Father, thank you that you are God who provides for your church, thank you that you gift your church. Help us to know what you've given to us and to use all the gifts that you've given to us by your Spirit to serve each other, that your church may grow into the fullness of Christ who is our head. Bless this message with your blessing tonight, take us home safely, praying in Jesus' name. Amen.*
